title: The Politics of ‘Terrorism’ as a Designation in Eastern DRC | African Arguments
gist: The author argues the 2021 'terrorist'/IS-affiliate designation of the Allied Democratic Forces in eastern DRC has been exploited - notably by Uganda - to legitimise a military presence in gold-rich, oil-adjacent territory, while obscuring the ADF's real local drivers (land disputes, cross-border trade, forced conscription) and locking in a counterterrorism response architecture that has made civilian violence worse.

insights.claim: State actors apply the 'terrorist' label as justification for excessive force against enemies, giving them powerful incentives to keep using a designation that has become analytically meaningless.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: A framework for decoding how counterterrorism labels mask resource and security agendas, applicable across conflict zones.

insights.claim: ADF's 2019 IS recognition as Central Africa Province solved two problems simultaneously - securing external financing and letting leader Seka Musa Baluku consolidate authority - while the relationship was about mutual reputational benefit rather than operational command and control.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Distinguishing branding from integration matters for calibrating the actual transnational-terror threat versus local insurgency.
insights.quote: The relationship has always been less about command and control than about mutual reputational benefit

insights.claim: Uganda has systematically inflated the ADF threat to justify arrests, deflect scrutiny of security-force abuses, extend the label to political opponents, and position itself as an indispensable US war-on-terror partner unlocking military training and financing.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Reveals the patronage incentives sustaining a self-perpetuating conflict, key to assessing regional military dynamics.
insights.quote: Uganda has been the designation’s most consequential exploiter.

insights.claim: Under Operation Shujaa, Ugandan forces concentrated in gold-rich areas of Ituri, Beni and Lubero along Ugandan-financed road corridors near Lake Albert oil infrastructure, with troop presence roughly doubling to some 6,000 by 2025.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that the deployment tracks gold and oil interests, signalling resource-extraction motives behind the security framing.
insights.quote: Ugandan forces have concentrated in gold-rich areas of Ituri, Beni, and Lubero
insights.locator: The label also provided legal and political cover

insights.claim: In June 2025 Uganda's top command detained senior military intelligence officers alleged to have orchestrated two Kampala suicide bombings with ADF operatives to secure funds and political leverage.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Suggests the threat may at times be manufactured by those tasked to defeat it, a corruption-of-counterterrorism signal.
insights.locator: This logic of amplification became evident

insights.claim: Kinshasa has attributed mass killings in Beni almost exclusively to the ADF since 2014 despite evidence implicating the Congolese army, finding the ADF a more internationally projectable threat than confronting the FDLR.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: The 'ADF violence' brand conceals army abuses and local score-settling, distorting which violence becomes investigable.
insights.quote: “ADF violence” has become a brand appropriated by criminal networks, local militias, and security forces
insights.locator: Kinshasa has run its own version

insights.claim: The ADF's resilience derives from its embeddedness in the Uganda-DRC borderlands - taxing farmers, trading cacao and gold, settling land disputes, recruiting through forced conscription and economic lure - far more than from transnational jihadism.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Correct diagnosis of local drivers implies counterterrorism-only responses will keep failing.
insights.quote: The ADF’s resilience has far more to do with its embeddedness in the Uganda-DRC borderlands than with transnational jihadism.

insights.claim: In the first five months of 2026 ADF attacks killed hundreds of civilians across Ituri and North Kivu, with Amnesty International reporting a surge in war crimes, and the group is more active now than when joint Ugandan-Congolese operations began in 2021.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: Hard evidence the military-first architecture is counterproductive, a basis for predicting continued deterioration absent policy change.

insights.claim: Demobilisation programmes have been starved of political support because the terrorist designation made negotiation legally and politically difficult, narrowing exit options for ADF members.
insights.speaker: Niko Berghmans
insights.so_what: The designation forecloses off-ramps, structurally prolonging the insurgency.
